Got the basics working in the new `toml` branch/PR. Check out the new README for details: 

https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/blob/4230ddf91f25e5ef33eed88d12f0bca672818214/README.rst

Since the idea for this and the syntax came from me, here is my thoughts:

The first thing to remember is that this is *not* a replacement for ``setup.py`` and it is *not* a mechanism by which to build a package. The sole purpose of this file is the same purpose as ``requirements.txt``, which is to make it possible to (re)create an environment with a particular set of installed packages. The most typical case you'd want to do this is if you have a particular instance of a Python application that you want to deploy. Thus any project that is publishing sdists and Wheels to PyPI will not have a mandatory ``Pipfile`` (they have one that they use for say, development that includes additional development utilities). It would be a good idea to read Yahuda Katz's [Clarifying the Roles of the .gemspec and Gemfile](http://yehudakatz.com/2010/12/16/clarifying-the-roles-of-the-gemspec-and-gemfile/) blog post and just mentally replace ``Gemfile`` with ``Pipfile``, ``Gemfile.lock`` with ``Pipfile.lock``, and ``.gemspec`` with ``setup.py``.

There are a few major problems with ``requirements.txt`` currently. The ones that I can think of off the top of my head are:

* The file format is incredibly simplistic which makes the simple case very simple, but the complex case quite annoying. For instance, trying to shoe horn in the new hashes in pip 8 very quickly leads to multi line disasters like Warehouse's [requirements/main.txt](https://github.com/pypa/warehouse/blob/master/requirements/main.txt). You can also see this in things like how we attempt to cram more and more information into the url structure of a non named dependency (e.g. ``git+https://github.com/dstufft/pipfile.git@branch#egg=pipfile-1.0&subdirectory=mysubdir``) and also in how we handle things like project specific build parameters (e.g. ``--install-option``) and also in our environment markes. The current file format does not lend itself to a sane way to express option, named parameters to the dependency and that leads to some very crazy lines in the current format.
* The format exists in a weird in between state where sometimes people use it with broad generic dependencies that represent the expected version ranges of the top level items they care about in recreating a specific environment and sometimes people use it with extremely locked down versions in order to exactly recreate a specific environment. Both have issues though.
  * If you're simply providing your top level requirements, then you're not likely using ``requirements.txt`` to your full benefit. This can make sense if you've got something that you expect people to only clone and then create a brand new environment but that shouldn't be your default case. This is the thing that ``requirements.txt`` makes easiest.
  * If you're version pinning every dependency (which you should in the common case) you're losing out on pip doing resolution for you. You have to go and manually manage dependencies, know when to remove some sub-dependency because it is no longer required, etc. There are a few mechanisms people have used to try and work around this like install and then ``pip freeze`` (although if you don't use a fresh environment you risk pulling in unrelated stuff you just happened to have installed) or using something like ``pip-compile``.
* The ``requirements.txt`` format appears deceptively simple to parse, leading people to do things like read it in their ``setup.py`` or developing tools that do that like [pbr](https://pypi.org/project/pbr/) that don't (and cannot) actually support everything that requirements files support leaving those projects to either error out or silently ignore directives.
* It is difficult to add additional new features to ``requirements.txt`` such as the ability to have named groups of dependencies because of the overly simplistic file format does not provide a reasonable way to add it.

Given all of the above, I set out to design a new format that would solve those issues. At the time I came up with three distinct options:

```python
# Proposal #1
#  This attempts to closely match the Gemfiles from Bundler, this might mean that
#   people would find it easier to pick up. However it could also mean that people
#   will think down of it as a "copy" and the differences might be noticed more since
#   The syntax is matched better.
source "https://simple.crate.io"
source "https://pypi.python.org/simple/"

dist "requests"
dist "Django", "~>1.4"
dist "pinax", git = "git://github.com/pinax/pinax.git", branch = "1.4"
dist "crate", path = "~/blech"
dist "test", group = "development"
dist "test2", group = ["development", "testing"]

group "development":
    dist "blah"
```

```python
# Proposal #2
#  This proposal takes the ideas from a Gemfile, and makes them more "Pythonic".
#   Instead of creating a DSL, simple functions are used. This shares a lot of the
#   same benefits as #1 in that people might find it easier to pick up as some will
#   already know Gemfiles. An obvious negative is that it tends to be a bit more verbose
#   than #1.
source("https://simple.crate.io")
source("https://pypi.python.org/simple/")

dist("requests")
dist("Django", "~>1.4")
dist("pinax", git="git://github.com/pinax/pinax.git", branch="1.4")
dist("crate", path="~/blech")
dist("test", group="development")
dist("test2", group=["development", "testing"])


group("development",
    dist("blah"),
)
```

```yaml
# Proposal #3
# This proposal takes the power introduced via 1 and 2, but uses yaml to create a
#  (hopefully) easy to parse set of requirements. The given example makes heavy
#  use of inline style of declaration so that one line == one dependency. One area of
#  concern is how to handle the "default" group. It will need to exist somehow. As a simpler
#  way here the default group can be defined outside of a groups: tag, but the question remains
#  what should that tag be called?
sources:
  - https://simple.crate.io
  - https://pypi.python.org/simple/

require:
  - requests
  - [Django, ~>1.4]
  - [Pinax, {git: "git://github.com/pinax/pinax.git", branch: 1.4}]
  - [crate, {path: ~/blech}]
  - [test, {group: development}]
  - [test2, {group: [development, testing]}]

groups:
  development:
    - blah
```

My thoughts at the time were:

> After writing these down and looking at them, my thoughts so far:
>
> 1. No. This doesn't look like it belongs anywhere near a Python application.
> 2. I'm on the fence about this one. It definitely looks like Python, and it would be easy to implement, however I worry that the fact it is/looks like Python would make people try to program in things that don't belong in this kind of file. On the other hand this file shouldn't ever get used as something like PyPI metadata so maybe the extra power would be useful.
> 3. I like how clean this looks. I worry about finding a good name for the "require" (maybe "require" is fine?). I don't know exactly how I feel about the inline syntax but there's no denying it looks cleaner than (2).

Since then I had picked up on the second option and refined it to look like:

```python
# This is designed to be edited by hand by python developers
# --index-url and friends look like command options, non inutive. No extra metadata available
source("https://simple.crate.io/")
source("https://pypi.python.org/simple/", verify_ssl=False)


# Design:
#  - Use real datastructures, make things clearer
#  - Seperate package name from version, using real strings.
#      Django==1.4 is a valid PyPI package, uninstallable from current files
#  - using kwargs creates a great way to provide optional options on a line by line basis
#     that python programmers are already familar with
#  - People should only have to worry about the things they depend on, the installer
#      should do the right thing
#  - People have different dependency based on environment
#  - Allow advanced usage for "wierd use cases" or "patterns not anticipated"

# Concerns:
#  - Using Python file might cause the same problems as setup.py
#    - This File not designed to be directly executed
#    - setup.py is typically sent to other people, requirements are typically for internal use
#    - Final result is still deterministic
#  - Somewhat more verbose
#    - Uses a syntax familar with all python programmers


dist("requests")  # Install the latest version of requests, and all dependency's
dist("Django", "==1.4")  # Install a version of Django matching the "==1.4" version spec and all dependencies
dist("pinax", git="git://github.com/pinax/pinax.git", branch="1.4") # Install pinax, using git and the 1.4 branch
dist("crate", path="~/blech", editable=True) # Install crate from the supplied path, and install it as an editable
dist("test", group="development")  # install test, but only if the development group is passed
dist("test2", group=["development", "testing"])  # install test2, but only if the development or testing group is passed

with group("development"):  # start a context that is equivilant to passing everything a certain group
    dist("another thing")   # install ``another thing`` if the development group is passed

with source("https://inhouse.example.com/"):
    # Things in here MUST be installed from the above source, the idea being that if you have forked, e.g. django-model-utils
    # you can uplaod to an inhouse server and force django-model-utils to install from this source, even in the case
    # there is another version higher then yours, or even the same version.
    # Additionally if packages installed inside of a with source(): have dependencies their deps are searched for
    # on source, and installed from there if possible. However if they are not available dependencies will fall back
    # to the global source()es.
    dist("django-model-utils")  # Not shown in the json lockfile.

# Details
#  - This file does NOT cause anything to directly get installed, the program uses it to build up an internal list of requirements
#  - All dist()'s are considered for the requirements, even if they are not going to get installed (e.g. they are in a not currently active group)
#    - This will allow things to work smoothly where the requirement in a group might modify the final installed version (e.g. a group might pin to Django<1.4)
#  - This file will be "compiled" down to json.
#    - When the compiled json exists, there is no need to do any sort of version resolving. We know exactly what it is we want to install. (e.g. --no-deps etc)
#    - We still need to find out where to download the packages from? (Unless we encode that in the json. Might be risky)
#  - If there's a corner case not thought of, file is still Python and allows people to easily extend
#  - You don't need to pin to exact versions, you just need to pin to what you want to support. e.g. if a pacakge follows semver, you could do package>1.2.1,<1.3 (you first started using the 1.2.1 version, and you expect 1.2.X to always be good for you.
#    - Exact versions are pinned automatically in the json file
```

Which is basically what you see as the idea of ``Pipfile`` now, modulo any adjusts that @kennethreitz has made.

My reasoning for going with Python instead of one of the other options are:

* The syntax is something that's going to be familiar to all Python developers, so other than learning the "API" used in the file they don't need to learn anything special outside of that.
* It provides good support for optional, named parameters that don't feel janky (e.g. adding them to the YAML option requires switching to using an inline list or dictionary or something or you make the common case much harder).
* It's more flexible which means things like ``with source()`` and ``with group()`` are more easily done *and* they can be more easily combined without having to add an explicit way of it.
* This file is generally not a security sensitive one, it's not something you're going to get from PyPI, it's not something that you're likely to be fetching from random people and executing.
  * At the time I originally spec'd this out projects like pyup.io, requires.io, etc didn't really exist so the only time this file was getting used was in an installation context.
* Making it "Just Python" allows people to shim in things that we maybe forgot to add and then we can look at baking in more proper support down the road.

So where do we go from here? I see a few possible answers:

* We just forge on ahead with the current assumptions and make a few of the not-primary cases harder (with the benefit that most of those cases are centered in other tools that can solve the problem once for their users, while ``requirement.txt``s current problems push the problems onto end users of this file).
* We come up with something that is *not* Python like using YAML or something like it.
* We make something that is Python like, but isn't actually Python. I think this would end up being a restricted subset of Python that is "safe". By safe I mean, not executing the file at all but parsing it using the AST parser and using that to extract out the information we need.
  * This gets tricky because the AST isn't going to provide us any of the runtime pieces of Python. Things like declaring a variable and then using them later on would need to either be coded ourselves to try and mimc what Python does or unsupported as not part of our restricted set of Python code.

Personally I think the correct way forward right now is to just roll with the Python syntax to nail down the API and execute the file in order to get the information out of it. However we should do the things mentioned by @ncoghlan in #6 to discourage people away from doing things that make this file not declarative. Before we make this a final thing that people actually are expected to use we can refactor out the ``exec()`` step and move to an ``AST`` based parser if we want to do that.

@jaraco and other setuptools maintainers I sincerely apologise for my comment, and the strife it has caused.

----

I've been getting a lot of flack for my comment above, especially on the Twitter sphere, and in private via email. Lot of people saying people should have known to pin the package or anything along those lines.

Let me state up front that when I posted that comment I was incredibly frustrated, mainly because it wasn't until this started happening that I found out that `virtualenv` by default -- upon creation of a new virtualenv -- will happily go fetch and download the latest version from `pypi` and install it. Everything else in production at $WORK is pinned to a private PyPI server that contains the packages we want with the versions we want, however unlike `python3 -mvenv` there was no realisation (from anyone on the team) that `virtualenv` would go download stuff from the internet.

Ansible with a pip statement will run `virtualenv` if you provide a virtualenv path, there is no flag to disable the default behaviour (to for example add `--no-download`), tox uses virtualenv internally, there is no way to pass `--no-download`. There is no way to specify with the `virtualenv` command to go fetch from an alternative PyPI location (`-i` flag is missing, I've opened an issue for this) other than exporting an environment flag that is not well documented (and happens to be consumed by `pip` during a run of `virtualenv`).

The `virtualenv` usage docs: https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/stable/userguide/#usage don't specify it is going to download code from the internet. I was fully expecting it to deploy with the `setuptools` it bundles.

Sure there are work-arounds, and I am sure there is a place in the `virtualenv` docs where it describes that by default it will go fetch things from PyPI, but the amount of long-time Python developers that were not aware of the default `virtualenv` behaviour within not just my team but people that I've spoken to in the community makes this a much bigger problem.

----

I maintain a variety of open source projects, I know how thankless of a job it is, and had I put on my maintainer hat when I posted the comment above I wouldn't have hit the comment button, I don't envy the position that `setuptools` has by being such an important part of the Python eco-system and thus being in a place where breakage is felt so far and wide.

The same reasons for rejecting this suggestion that were given in the linked bug report for venv apply here as well:

1. Virtual environments aren't meant to be moved, they are cheap to create and the recommended approach if you want to move an environment is simply to recreate it in the new location.
2. Packages installed into a virtualenv will have the absolute path to the Python interpreter hard coded into any entry point wrappers that get installed, so moving the environment would break these even if the activate script was modified as suggested.

I just started to write changelog entry when question about parameter names arose. If we leave them as `watchdog_*`, no visible for users API changes need to be documented. But OK, lets rename them to `ping_*`, I like it more too :)

Problem is that yaml makes no difference between hashes (JS objects with simple keys) and maps. Option can only force to use maps everywhere, not in specific cases. But that makes result ugly for JS, as discussed in #169.

Sets are postponed, because maps are less important than maps.

To move forward, need to invent good solution what to do with maps, if people wish to work with hashes in most cases (even when maps used in some places of document).

1. Option name. First of all there's no such term as "scalar type" in YAML. The name is misleading. I think it should be named `valueFlowScalars` because it's about values, not keys and it's scope is flow scalars only. This name will allow us to conveniently add `keyFlowScalars` and `blockScalars` options in future.
2. The option must accept `plain` (default), `single`, `double` strings as possible values. STYLE_* constants are internal for the dumper. They are not exposed by public API currently, not exposed by this PR, and shouldn't be exposed at all.
3. `if (string.length === 0)` clause was placed after style resolver for absolutely no reason. It should remain were it was. Also I think it's body should be expressed via `?` syntax, not switch.
4. Supplied test doesn't test added feature at all. It tests dumper correctness for the second time while it should ensure that selected scalar styles applied correctly.
5. No documentation entry for new option in README.

The PR includes an intensity sort that addresses the second issue.

There's another problem with the max filter applied at the top of the function. The exclusion neighbourhood values ```min_distance``` and ```min_angle``` are being used as the window for the 1d max filters. The purpose of this filter (I believe) is to expand the peak pixels for the subsequent label. It is not meant to define the suppression windows, that happens later.

Consider what happens when I have three peaks of intensities 1,2,3 equally spaced by 10 units. If I set ``` min_distance=11``` I expect to receive peaks 1 and 3 since 3 suppresses 2, but not 1. However, because of the max filter 2 ends up suppressing 1 because the it fails the mask test. Peak 1 never even shows up in the ```coords``` list.

It seems to me that ```distance_size``` and ```angle_size``` should not be dependant on the values for ```min_distance``` and ```min_angle```. To fix it I've just hardcoded them to something small. I'm not sure whether this is the correct solution. Would it be better to have two more arguments?

Finally, using max has the effect of not preserving integrated intensity. Two nearby, equally spaced peaks will be maxed to a single peak having the same intensity, not the sum. It seems to me that each labelled region should have the integrated intensity from the area covered by the label.

@odebeir the patchc now simply makes the warning optional.
I don't like optional warnings without explanations, but with the comment pointing to the issue, I think it is fine.

I agree with @ahojnnes interpretation and that our default should be `preserve_range=True`. Our contract with our users should be something along the lines of, "we will not mess with your data unless absolutely necessary". This includes both clipping and scaling.

@JDWarner in addition, I don't think iterating over a tuple is a good plan at all. Very often, the additional returned arrays of a function are not images at all but things like transformations, indexes, etc. Clipping in these scenarios would be disastrous.

@nharraud My basic target were packages that were "never" actively used, for some epsilon definition of "never".  E.g. `jsonalchemy` wasn't even released on PyPI yet.

Perhaps we should simply distinguish two cases and make a list of packages which should go for "gentle" obsolescence path, and which can go for "hard" obsolescence path.  An example of guiding principles I had roughly in mind: 

- some production releases on PyPI -> option 1 
- some pre-production releases on PyPI, some active users still -> option 1
- some pre-production releases on PyPI, no active users anymore -> option 3 
- no releases on PyPI -> option 3

I have reviewed code and docs a bit more. zope.testrunner keeps it simple for itself.
They do not state if a layer has to be a class, but they state that Layers can be extended giving an example of extending classes via subclassing. http://docs.zope.org/zope.testrunner/index.html#layers
In the implementation, base classes are looked up by going over the elements of the `__bases__` property which is a python property. https://docs.python.org/2/library/stdtypes.html#special-attributes
Since it is not part of the zope.testrunner docs, I would say that it is reasonable to expect a layer to be a class like attribute. If one wants to fake it, depending on internal knowledge of how resolution works, one has to suck it up that a new patch release changes internal details and one has to provide a __mro__ method too now.
Sorting by mro is safe because layer setup is done breath-first. __mro__ works the same, mostly.

Hi, I am not a Windows user, but I'll try to give you some suggestions.

First of all, the package that needs HDF5 is PyTables, so to make things easier, I would download PyTables from source and try to install that separately (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tables). Once it is installed correctly, deepdish should work.

You will need to figure out where HDF5 was installed (search for it?). Once you know this, you can run the PyTables installation with:

    python setup.py install --hdf5=/path/to/hdf5

I guess that should probably be something like `C:\path\to\hdf5`, but you get my point. Alternatively, you might be able to add the HDF5 directory to the appropriate environment variable (on Linux, this would be `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, but on Windows it might be `PATH`, but I am not sure). Would another way to check if the locals dict is actually the global dict be to compare `f_locals` to `f_globals`?  If they're the same dict, then we don't want to clear.  For example:

```python
import sys
frame = sys._getframe(0)
fl = frame.f_locals
fg = frame.f_globals

print('f_locals is f_globals outside of function: ', fl is fg)

def test_func():
    frame = sys._getframe(0)
    fl = frame.f_locals
    fg = frame.f_globals
    
    print('f_locals is f_globals inside function: ', fl is fg)
    
test_func()
```
```
f_locals is f_globals outside of function:  True
f_locals is f_globals inside function:  False
```

Maybe it's equivalent to `call_frame.f_back is not None` in most (all?) cases, but the case where locals and globals are the same is what we really want to watch out for.

It is not expected behavior. If a test fails in the test case an error in teardown should not cause that failure to vanish. This was one of the #1 reasons to reexamine test result recording.
This is intentional. 1. test fail, teardown pass -> FAIL 2. teat fail, teardown error -> ERROR The "failure" is not "lost" in case 2. The termination signal is still the original failure, the details and stacktrace fields still use the original failure's info. But an error in teardown test *should* change the status of the test result because something unexpected happened in teardown. A test is tallied as FAIL only if an explicit test failure signal is thrown in test body and no other errors occurred in the test. If anything unexpected happen, the status is ERROR. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/284)*

fig2 = plt.figure()
ax2 = fig2.add_subplot(111)

for interpolant in ['linear', 'cubic', 'quintic',
                    'lanczos3', 'lanczos4', 'lanczos5',
                    'sinc']:

    # Fourier transform via drawKImage
    stepk = 2.0*np.pi/(Nx*scale)
    maxk = np.pi/scale
    ii = galsim.InterpolatedImage(img, calculate_stepk=stepk, calculate_maxk=maxk,
                                  x_interpolant=interpolant)
    re, im = ii.drawKImage(nx=Nx, ny=Nx, scale=stepk)
    drawKout = re.array + 1j * im.array
    # The alternating sign flips on the following line seems to help match the phase (but not
    # perfectly), though I don't know why.
    drawKout[::2, ::2] *= -1
    drawKk = (np.arange(Nx)-Nx2)*stepk

    # Fourier transform via np.fft
    fftout = np.fft.fft2(img.array)
    fftk = np.fft.fftfreq(Nx, scale) * 2.0 * np.pi
    fftout = np.fft.fftshift(fftout)  # Center k=(0,0)
    fftk = np.fft.fftshift(fftk)
    assert np.allclose(drawKk, fftk)  # Make sure we've queried the same modes.
    ax.plot(fftk, 1.0-np.abs(drawKout[Nx2, :])/np.abs(fftout[Nx2, :]), label=interpolant)

    phases = np.angle(drawKout[Nx2, :]) - np.angle(fftout[Nx2, :])
    phases = phases % (2 * np.pi)
    ax2.plot(fftk, phases, label=interpolant)
ax.set_xlim(0, maxk)
ax.set_ylim(-0.01, 0.01)
ax.legend(loc='best')
ax.set_ylabel("1 - abs(drawK) / abs(fft)")
ax.set_xlabel("k")
fig.show()

ax2.legend(loc='best')
ax2.set_ylabel("arg(drawK) - arg(fft)")
ax2.set_xlabel("k")
fig2.show()
plt.show()
```

Or how about [Sky Atlas with their 88 Twitter followers](https://twitter.com/skyatlascom) vs. [Monero's 1231 Twitter followers](https://twitter.com/monerocurrency)?

It would be unconscionable for these others to exist and then try and argue that Monero is somehow not worthy of consideration.

We have [a branding pack](https://downloads.getmonero.org/resources/branding.zip) available, but given that compiling one's own web font is no trivial task for the amateur web developer (and Font Awesome is widely available on popular CDNs) it stands to reason that the FA route would be more appealing.

I also can't agree with your argument that a Font Awesome icon is "advertising". Monero isn't a for-profit company that would benefit from having an icon. I doubt having a Font Awesome icon will make any difference at all. But regardless, Monero *is* a free, fair, open-source project with screeds of users all over the planet, and there is a very practical case to be made for a Font Awesome icon.

@tseaver Here is a proof of concept for running readable interpreter snippets without worrying about calling `eval` / `exec` etc.

First, create a fake module (or any object really) with our content in the `__doc__`

```python
>>> import types
>>> fake_mod = types.ModuleType('haha-nope')
>>> fake_mod.__doc__ = """\
... Here is some prose, and then a dragon appears
... and it makes sure division::
...
... >>> a = 45
... >>> a / 3
... 15
...
... and then the bunny hops down the lane and we
... find out we have to print a null::
...
... >>> b = {}
... >>> v = b.get('anything')
... >>> v
... >>> print(v)
... None
... """
```

then hand that object off to `doctest.run_docstring_example`

```python
>>> import doctest
>>> fake_globals = {}
>>> doctest.run_docstring_examples(fake_mod, fake_globals, verbose=False, name='foo')
>>> doctest.run_docstring_examples(fake_mod, fake_globals, verbose=True, name='foo')
Finding tests in foo

Expecting nothing
ok

    a / 3
Expecting:
    15
ok

    b = {}
Expecting nothing
ok

    v = b.get('anything')
Expecting nothing
ok

ok

> the library cares more about some trend from last week than doing what you'd expect the simplest possible HTTP client

I don't think that's called for, though I do appreciate you talking the time to file the issue. I designed `google-resumable-media-python`, so you can put the blame 100% on my shoulders.

The sans-I/O approach has nothing to do with the "missing feature" of piping the HTTP payload directly into a file. What's more, the behavior of the **old** code (i.e. `google.cloud.streaming`) is mostly the same, and that was written 3+ years ago.

The real issue is that the `google-cloud-storage` library doesn't do some kind of "smart chunking". It's definitely worth discussing. The reason there is no smart chunking is because we may not "a priori" know the size of the file, and it would be an **extra** round trip to determine the size before the first request. (Right now the `google-cloud-storage` package does chunked or full download, depending [only on the chunk size][2].)

We have [discussed][1] strategies to reduce the amount of a file that ends up in RAM, but haven't had a chance to implement it yet.

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-resumable-media-python/issues/5
[2]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/ce9cd83e6a65212c7e26a41344d38a53c770c2b7/storage/google/cloud/storage/blob.py#L420-L426

The low level GAPIC retries don't work for streaming RPCs like Cloud Bigtable Read Rows.  Default retries just retry the original request until it completes.  Retries for streaming RPCs need to pick up from where they previously left off, which requires custom logic.  We actually put in a fix for this 4 months ago with PR #4882.

> Our goal is to catch/wrap and raise the same exception for either backend.

This goal seems very odd to me. You have two different _transports_, not two different backends. Is it really true that the same operation causes different errors to be raised, assuming the error is bubbled up completely? One transport's status code semantics (HTTP) constrain you in a way that causes ambiguity, which I understand, but as the client library, you are on the hook to disambiguate that in a way that is idiomatic in the language at hand. An exception that has different meaning based on its state _post-instantiation_ is not Pythonic, no?

What is `KEYPROPERTY`? ISTM that it is just a [`Key`][1]. If that's the case, [`id` is a `@property`][2] which means you need to call `field.id` not `field.id()`. Since a `@property`, `field.id` will return an integer, which can't be called. For example:

```python
>>> n = 1
>>> n()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: 'int' object is not callable
>>>
>>> n = 1L
>>> n()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: 'long' object is not callable
```

Please re-open if I'm mistaken.

[2]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/5a0e492b4098fe3c2df4d1821a432cbaee15edd6/datastore/google/cloud/datastore/key.py#L306

I want to say great work first and foremost.
Thank you for giving me an opportunity to just raise some questions. 

I realize the firebase client libraries are really designed to be idiomatic. However, after using some of the other SDK's with similar APIs it was a little unexpected to see an exception here.

I would agree that in python raising an exception is the standard idiom and `EAFP` is a good method to live by if the code you're executing is prone to `exceptions`. But if we know there is only a single possible error `NotFound` then it feels a little heavy handed to wrap in a `try:catch`.

`LBYL` feels like the better option here.

But I'm not proposing you change the default behavior, I'm simply asking if it makes sense to add a keyword argument to `ignore_notfound`

I would expect an example like this to work well if we simply returned a `document snapshot` that contained an empty dictionary and `exists: False`. Pythons `dictionary.get` allows me to set the default value without throwing an exception.

Having to introduce a `try:catch` also introduces a write really early on in a transaction that doesn't support read after write.

```python
@firestore.transactional
def debit_account(tx, account, amount):
    walletRef = wallets.document(account)
    walletSnap = walletRef.get(transaction=tx, ignore_notfound=True)
    wallet = walletSnap.to_dict()
    tokens = wallet.get('tokens', 0)

    if amount < 0 and tokens < abs(amount):
        return WalletCodes.INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS

    tokens += amount

    tx.set(walletRef, {
        'tokens': tokens
    }, firestore.CreateIfMissingOption(True))

    return WalletCodes.SUCCESSFUL_DEBIT
```

`docsnap` has a property `exists`

```js
let docsnap = await cityRef.get()
```

### Java
`docsnap` has a method `exists` 
```java
DocumentSnapshot docsnap = future.get()
```

### Go
`docsnap` is an empty interface and `err` can be simply ignored
```go
docsnap, _ := client.Doc("Wallets/{UID}").Get(ctx)
```

It's been a very longstanding feature request, and has frequently bitten people when manually partitioning Test classes into different targets. As an example, `./pants list tests/::` in the pantsbuild pants repo indicates that there may be 21 test targets without any matching sources (and all the ones that I eyeballed actually did have sources that weren't matched by their globs).

> TBH I'm not even sure that I agree that a target with no sources indicates a bug.

A target with no sources either 1) needn't exist, or 2) has a literal bug in that it's failing to build a thing.

> E.g., I have a set of standard globs for web resources (*.css, *.js) that I copy-paste everywhere, without having to worry about whether each specific location has both .css and .js files.

Encouraging copy-pasting of targets doesn't seem like it should be a high priority goal... can we encourage factoring out the duplication instead?

The KCL attempts to retrieve records using a timed process.  The value of `idleTimeBetweenReadsInMillis` controls how long it will sleep between calls to Kinesis.  If it gets no records on a call it extends the amount of time it will sleep.  The amount of time the record processor takes to process records can also slow down the rate the KCL polls for records from Kinesis. So sleeping isn't necessarily a problem unless you're falling behind.

You can check approximately how far behind the KCL thinks it is by looking at the value of [`millis_behind_latest`](https://github.com/awslabs/amazon-kinesis-client-python/blob/master/amazon_kclpy/messages.py#L133) on the process records input.  This requires that you use the v2 record process base class: [`RecordProcessorBase`](https://github.com/awslabs/amazon-kinesis-client-python/blob/master/amazon_kclpy/v2/processor.py#L17).

Hi Brian,

regarding your first question: `<bucket>` in line 10 can be related to `Bucket` in line 19, but must not. What you define in line 10 is where your code artifact for your Lambda function can be picked up by `aws cloudformation package` (see also my answer to your second question below). The `Bucket` in line 19 defines a bucket which is able to call your Lambda function if a new object is created in that bucket.

Regarding your second question: you use `CodeUri` to define where you're artifacts are at the moment and where the AWS cli can pick them up when calling `aws cloudformation package`. `--s3-bucket` is then used for those artifacts which are e.g. on your local machine, so they are uploaded to S3 and can be referenced (because AWS has no access to your local machine obviously).

I hope I could help you with this.

Cheers,
Sebastian

> # Plan:
> I'm eager to continue, so I drafted a plan for the next steps that are required:
> - [ ]  Feature docs
> - [ ]  Functional tests
> - [ ]  Unit tests
> - [ ]  Doc strings

A couple remarks on naming, just so we use the established vocabulary:

* The first one here is either the "analysis page" or the "enhancement proposal" (like a PEP). I generally call it the analysis page. It's always one page, and at this stage its all about capturing the analysis behind the feature (group), so it seems an apt enough name. In any case, it's the term you'll see used over and over again in the project conversation history.

* We've used the term "acceptance tests" for the ones written in Cucumber and run with Behave. I think that's kind of common for Cucumber language tests, but in any case it's how you'll see them referred to in the conversation history. 

> ## Analysis page
> I've committed an initial version, I'll update it according the current implementation, but I think it's already more or less okay.

I think we've got a good start there, actually probably well toward 80%. Where we're weak is in the specifics of the protocol (which determine the API calls to be implemented, so are critical) and scrubbing through the conversation here to pull out the experimental details, like overlapping is okay, elements with same ID trigger repair error, etc. I'll have another look once you've updated it as far as you can. I expect we'll have some more conversation surrounding the protocol section.

> ## Acceptance tests
> These tests are run using behave, I haven't used this before, but there are many examples in the package. However, I think we need to agree on which `feature` files are going to be required, since this implementation is accross the `document`, `paragraph` and `run` objects.
>
> As with all other things in this package, my guess is that it needs to be thorough ;) therefore I think we require the following `feature` files:
> -  [ ] run-add_bookmark_start.feature
> -  [ ] run-add_bookmark_end.feature
> -  [ ] par-add_bookmark_start.feature
> -  [ ] par-add_bookmark_end.feature
> -  [ ] doc-add_bookmark_start.feature
> -  [ ] doc-add_bookmark_end.feature

Let's just put them all in `bookmark.feature` for now. I've gone back and forth and back again on how to organize acceptance tests, but where I've landed for now is put everything associated with an object type in the same feature. If it gets over a couple hundred lines, we'll find a way to break it apart neatly.

There are a lot of moves to figure out for yourself when starting here, so I propose we start with the following single acceptance test and then we can see where we are when that one's passing; sometimes I add new ones in groups.

Actually, this one should go in `doc-access-collections.feature` so you won't even need to create a new file initially:

```cucumber
Given a Document object as document
 Then document.bookmarks is a Bookmarks object
```

> ## Unit-tests
> Since we touched upon many other files, I think this will be a bit of work as well, or maybe you think of a more elegant way to implement the bookmark code, but there will be additions required to the following unittest files:
> -  [ ] \tests\test_document.py
> -  [ ] \tests\oxml\test_run.py
> -  [ ] \tests\test_document.py
> -  [ ] \tests\oxml\text.py
> -  [ ] \tests\text\test_run.py
> -  [ ] \tests\text\test_paragraph.py
> 
> And a new file should be added:
> 
> -  [ ] \tests\text\test_bookmark.py
> 
> However, there seem to be some tests that are failing? 

You'll be developing *test-driven* (as opposed to spiking, which you can feel free to continue to do). One consequence of this is you don't need to try to figure out what the unit tests will look like as a whole; they will emerge in a natural pattern as you go.

Another consequence is that you will be *inserting* your acceptance test commit immediately after your analysis page commit. So while unit tests may be failing on your spike tip, they will not fail on your test-driven (TDD) tip. The acceptance test will be decorated with `@wip` so it can be skipped until it passes and doesn't count as a failing test for interim purposes.

When your acceptance test is run, it will fail (or it's not a good test). *Where* it fails leads you to the first unit test. Let's get to that when we see it. I expect it will give rise to a commit named: `doc: add Document.bookmarks`.

> ## Docstrings
> You mentioned that you've adopted a certain style of docstring? Anyway, I think I need to add the docstrings in one commit, and you can review them in one go.

You can add these into your spike if you want, I don't mind either way. Where they will be *required*, is in every commit up to your TDD tip.

cap = imageio.get_reader(path)

Hello, thanks for the report!

We'll look into this and push out a fix if necessary by the next AdWords release.

> So shouldn't we report any bugs found when using the library for you folks to investigate and escalate to the API layer if necessary?

No, we don't investigate API issues on this issue tracker. I'd actually suggest the opposite; if you're not sure if the underlying issue is related to the API, start at the API support forum and escalate to the specific library's issue tracker if it is found to be an issue directly related to that library. The reasoning for this is that if the underlying API has an issue, it's likely not possible for us to resolve your issue at the client library level--e.g. it wouldn't be possible for us to add a field that doesn't exist in the API response.

In hindsight though, there is one quick way to determine whether this is an API or client library issue. Assuming that you're using `ParseResponse`, you should be passing in the response XML. Does the response XML contain this field? If not, then it is definitely related to the API rather than this client library.

Regards,
Mark

Just wanted to leave a note here for @josele86 and others that, although at the moment `googleads` remains pinned to `oauth2client==1.5.2` it is possible as of the latest `google-api-python-client` to work around the issue of mutually incompatible oauth2client installations between the two libraries. Although google-api-python-client requires the latest oauth2client by default, it is backwards-compatible with 1.5.2. For the workaround, you just have to be careful to install googleads with pip _after_ google-api-python-client, so you end up with 1.5.2 instead of a newer version.

Thanks for the details of the problem.  A PanDeviceXapiError typically indicates an error message coming from the device as the result of an API call.  In this case, the Panorama is replying with a message "Illegal parameter".

The cause of this error is actually due to how the command is formed when you call the .op() method.  To convert the command correctly for the API call, the job id must be in double quotations.  For example:

    # notice the double quotes around 4936
    print pano.op('show jobs id "4936"', xml=True)

The reason the quotation marks are needed is explained in the pan-python documentation (pan-python is a dependency of pandevice):
https://github.com/kevinsteves/pan-python/blob/master/doc/pan.xapi.rst#opcmdnone-vsysnone-cmd_xmlfalse

In that doc, it explains that the command is converted to XML before it gets passed to the device.  Parts of the command XML must be XML tags, while other parts are text within tags.  Anything inside double quotes will be treated as text within a tag, so the correctly converted command XML should look like this:

`<show><jobs><id>4936</id></jobs></show>`

Optionally you can pass the XML in directly, bypassing conversion to XML, by setting cmd_xml to False.  For example:

    # this also works
    print pano.op('<show><jobs><id>4936</id></jobs></show>', xml=True, cmd_xml=False)

That way the library is expecting XML and won't attempt the conversion to XML before making the API call to the device.

**tl;dr:**   In summary, the easiest solution is to change the method call by putting double quotations around the job id:

    print pano.op('show jobs id "4936"', xml=True)

- cover all classes with hash/cmp implementations that allow proper testing of equality of objects and using them in sets or as dict keys  (implementing cmp alone allows to not implement eq and neq, Python will derive eq and neq from the result of cmp).

I very much think we should crash, not just give a warning, on initial model loading if we can't find an embedding file that you're wanting to use.  That's a fatal error.  Especially given how much info we spew out at the beginning of training, you'd almost certainly miss the warning, anyway.  And we _don't_ want to rely on the embedding file being there if you're loading a model - the weights are already saved in your weights file, and that introduces a really crazy runtime dependency, so in order to use our trained models you first have to download glove.  So we _have_ to have some kind of switch so that the model specification behaves slightly differently at train time vs. at load time.

I kind of like Joel's suggestion of just letting `Model.load()` handle this complexity, so that we don't have to muddy the rest of the API to handle this case.  One issue is that I'm thinking of adding some parameters that are only valid if you're using pre-trained embeddings, and you kind of want to remove all of those at the same time, which makes this a bit complicated...  But it's probably better than what I did here.

Calling `conda` command out of git repo root uses the installed one. This is coherent with the message printed by `setup.py`
```
WARNING: Your current install method for conda only supports conda
as a python library.  You are not installing a conda executable command
or activate/deactivate commands.  If your intention is to install conda
as a standalone application, currently supported install methods include
the Anaconda installer and the miniconda installer.  If you'd still like
for setup.py to create entry points for you, use `utils/setup-testing.py`.
```

Running `%CONDA%\python setup.py develop -u` in the second case, effectively restores the installation to the initial state.

May be this is worth documenting.

The big epiphanies leading up to conda 4.3.13 that finally made multi-user work were

1. Stop relying on directories to test permissions. Testing permissions on a single representative file that under normal conditions is a proxy for an entire group of paths is much more reliable. There are three "group types" that conda is generally concerned about w.r.t. permissions: prefixes, `pkgs` directories, and `envs` directories. Each has a "magic file" pointed to in conda/base/constants.py. The magic file for a conda prefix is `conda-meta/history`, and `pkgs/urls.txt` for the `pkgs` directory. In conda 4.4 I've added an `envs/catalog.json` that's the magic file for the `envs` directory (it also is now used for other meta information associated with that `envs` directory). 2. Stop relying on `os.stat` to test permissions. It's just not reliable across file systems and operating systems. Given use of (1), use `open(path, 'a').close()` to ultimately test for writability.

I realized that actually all three functions (`activate`, `deactivate`, and `reactivate`) all had cases where a `post` function was necessary.

So I back tracked a little and instead of splitting each function into two phases (e.g. `deactivate` and `deactivatepost`) I introduced a new function called `post`. The `post` function relies on a new environment variable `$CONDA_POST` which is a `old_prefix:new_prefix` syntax. This then allows the `post` command to only deal with fixing the `$PATH` after the necessary `deactivate.d` scripts have been called (as well as unsetting `$CONDA_POST`).

I was mistaken about the environment and it appears that conda version 4.0.1 was in my miniconda install, so I was able to proceed. @kalefranz's suggestion above did it for me. The issue is that the buildpack environment is ephemeral until a build succeeds, then it stays around. It was difficult to get the miniconda environment printed out while the buildpack executes, but for others who may hit this problem, I would suggest adding something like:

```
conda install conda=4.0.1
```
Just above this line:

https://github.com/kennethreitz/conda-buildpack/blob/master/bin/steps/conda_compile#L13

If your application has been around for a while, it's likely you have that version of conda in your cache.

Looked into this a bit further. Appears this is in fact a file conflict. `dir` is confusingly a file. This seems to be caused by `install-info` as noted in [this SO answer]( https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/73433 ). Also more details in [this doc]( https://www.gnu.org/software/texinfo/manual/texinfo/html_node/Installing-Dir-Entries.html ). Morale of the story is `install-info` actually amends this file as more documentation is installed like a TOC.

Guessing Anaconda 5 avoided this issue as this file was generated when building the compilers and as everything else builds off the compilers they would have this file in the environment already. Thus even if they amended the file, the changes would not have been kept (unless explicitly requested in the `conda-build` recipe). It's possible they save the changes in later packages, but have no idea ATM.

There are a couple fixes:

1. We rebuild with the new compilers and the conflict goes away, but the docs are lost.
2. Do 1 and we explicitly keep changes to `dir` keeping the docs in tact, but creating a bit of a headache when installing.
3. We simply delete the info docs any time a package creates them.
4. Do 1 and 2, but keep doc packages with this content so it doesn't affect users unless they request the docs.

There may be others, but any of these could work. Might also be worth looking into what other package managers do to solve this problem.

note:  This bug is with conda 4.4.6 and anaconda-navigator 1.6.11

### TL;DR: 
```
# use -p parameter to pass absolute path of \envs directory to conda env export
$ conda env export -p path/to/envs > env_file.yml
```
### Explanation
Assuming you install conda to a "custom" location using -p. 
When you go to export an environment using something like 
```
conda env export > env_file.yml
```
you will likely get a CondaEnvironmentNotFoundError: Could not find environment:

In order to fix this you can do a 
```
conda info --envs
```
to find the location of your conda environments, simply copy the prefix and supply to conda env export using -p parameter. 

Here is an example: 
First I list my environments to get the prefix 
```
(ds_python) cas@ubuntu:~/working_dir/python_r_ds$ conda info --envs
# conda environments:
#
ds_python             *  /home/cas/miniconda/envs/ds_python
root                     /home/cas/miniconda
```
After copying the path I can provide it to conda env export
```
(ds_python) cas@ubuntu:~/working_dir/python_r_ds$ conda env export -p /home/cas/miniconda/envs/ds_python > ds_python_env.yml
```

The macro argument is still semantically realtime. Inside the macro the realtime is divided with 1ns to get a ration which is real. Thus you should use a realtime such as "1us" as the argument before. The macro interface has not changed only the implementation to fix the bug that you discovered when the timescale of the test bench differed from the timescale used in vunit_pkg. 

 In verilog it seems realtime such as 1ns is just a number relative the timescale. Thus if I passed 1ns realtime from testbench to vunit_pkg it caused a different delay if different timescales are used in the files. Strange semantics of verilog that a literal such as 1ns is not an absolute time.

The minimum was important at the time because the original implementation didn't discriminate against connect (or busy) status.  Now that those distinctions exist, I think the _timeout minimum_ can be lifted.  

However, I tend to agree with @parente that an _interval minimum_ is still warranted.  If there are enough active kernels such that checking all for idle timeout exceeds the culling interval, then it seems as though the server could get into a pathological state of only culling (if I'm understanding how tornado works). 

What about lifting the timeout minimum, but introduce an interval minimum for the sole purpose of saturation prevention?  Yes, the interval minimum would then act as a logical timeout value.  However, this would also help satisfy the connection issues condition, which could be considered another pathological state example.

I guess I'm thinking we should impose at least a `10` second interval minimum.

This would get the URL of the running Jupyter Notebook server.

```python
import json
import re

import ipykernel
import notebook

import requests


def get_notebook_url():
    """
    Return the url of the current jupyter notebook.
    """
    kernel_id = re.search(
        "kernel-(.*).json",
        ipykernel.connect.get_connection_file()
    ).group(1)
    servers = notebook.notebookapp.list_running_servers()
    for s in servers:
        response = requests.get(
            requests.compat.urljoin(s["url"], "api/sessions"),
            params={"token": s.get("token", "")}
        )
        for nn in json.loads(response.text):
            if nn["kernel"]["id"] == kernel_id:
                return s["url"]
```

In the class you have

```python
amounts = ListType(StorageAmount, required=True)
```

The first argument to `ListType` should be a Schematics type, but `StorageAmount` seems to be a model. Since you're passing a class as the first argument, Schematics assumes it's a type class to be instantiated with the `required=True` argument. But a model doesn't take a `required` argument, hence the error. Perhaps you intended:

```python
amounts = ListType(ModelType(StorageAmount), required=True)
```

Right now, we only check if the first argument is an instance of `BaseType`. If not, the code assumes it's a type class. It would be nice to actually check that it is.

It's a flag indicating that the process was initiated from a model instance. It controls the behavior of submodels: if you're importing into a dictionary using the functional API, submodels will be imported as dictionaries as well. Conversely, using `Model.__init__` will recursively instatiate submodels too.

In fact, the flag *should* be present in the default validation context. The thing is, it only matters when you initiate the validation process from a standalone type. That is not yet officially supported usage, so it lacks testcases, as evidenced by the error you got.

I guess my opinion more or less comes down to "recursive modules sounds neat in theory, but high-risk in practice".

And having Peru play well with Makefiles is pretty high on my needs list, FWIW.

For example, one way to implement it could be to use one output layer (fully connected or convolutional, depending on the argument type) for each of the argument types listed in https://github.com/deepmind/pysc2/blob/master/pysc2/lib/actions.py#L200, as well as for the function identifier. During sampling, one would then first sample the function id from the predicted function identifier distribution, and then sample from the appropriate argument type distribution, depending on the argument types used in the arguments of this function type.

So you would have a single channel 1x1 conv output for each of the types 'screen', screen2', 'minimap', and the non-spatial arguments are similar. When computing the log probs and entropies, you can mask out the arguments which are not used for a specific action sample.

Refering to the paper, **4.2**:
>  In most of our experiments we found it sufficient
to model sub-actions independently, however, we also explored with auto-regressive policies where
the ordering was chosen such that we first decide for the function identifier, then all categorical
arguments and finally, if relevant, pixel coordinates.

The above would correspond to "modelling sub-actions independently".

Also, have a look at this issue for the input feature layers: https://github.com/deepmind/pysc2/issues/116.

Hi Andrew,

Thanks for submitting this PR. Given the way we handle passing state currently (as you mention above), I think this is probably a reasonable way to convey the row count from the `Cmd` to the `Cursor`.

A couple of quick notes regarding what could be done before merging, in my opinion:

- Looks like the [debug](https://github.com/capitalone/giraffez/pull/55/files#diff-7f0ff1e5ad3aac94b3f2c63ad378b89fR101) arg was left in but doesn't do anything at the moment
- You mention the DB API 2.0 specs in your original issue; they say to return -1 in the case that a `rowcount` is unavailable or can't be determined. This sounds like a good idea to me - it looks like `conn->rowcount` should probably be set to -1 on creation and whenever `teradata_execute` is called, does this logic sound right (to everyone)?
- Is there a failure case in decoding the `ActivityCount` attribute from the  success parcel? I haven't checked on that part of the docs recently, but I will now. Even if it is overkill it might be worth checking the length of the data anyway before decoding.
- It looks like `ActivityCount` and hence `rowcount` is an `uint32_t`, this should be used as the field type and for the temporary variable in `teradata_execute`. Of course this prohibits us from using -1 directly in that field as specified above: I would maybe suggest keeping some separate state (on the `conn` or otherwise) which indicated to `Cmd_rowcount` whether we are in a valid state for a row count or not, and checking that to determine if -1 should be returned. Definitely open to suggestion here, there might be something more straightforward to do.
- Docs for this change would be good to have, especially to document the "quirks" of behavior. I think the need for iterating the cursor object before `rowcount` will be populated warrants an example as well. If you are interested in adding this please feel free, otherwise I would be happy to update the docs.

Curious to hear thoughts from you and others, as there is a bit of nuance here. Thank you again!

@pieterb I've been meaning to toy with this idea for a while but I'm glad to see you've successfully attempted it 👍.

I would actually be very partial to developing the two projects in tandem and lean towards doing as @nicoddemus suggested:
> What if instead aiopluggy depends on pluggy as a package, subclassing the relevant parts and making them async, would that be a possibility? I think we are open to make some internal refactorings to facilitate/make it possible if that's the case.

If it were up to just me I'd say introduce your new stuff to the project now under an `async` package/folder that we can only import for python 3.5+.

Having `aiopluggy` more coupled with `pluggy` would probably make both projects better in the long run as we can consolidate ideas from the `async` design and vice-versa. It would also encourage more improvements to `pluggy` core as demanded by *modern python people* (cough).
`aiopluggy` is also likely to get more adoption if it stays api compatible with `pluggy` especially if discovered by the `pytest` community for async related plugins.

They way I would have done it:
- build `aiopluggy` off of `pluggy` instead of re-writing its components
- keep the same naming unless you want to suggest adding/modifying the api in `pluggy` itself

I would actually appreciate the help in pushing forward with new features (like an async api) that we can roll out in a backwards compatible way.

```python
from neat.parallel import ParallelEvaluator
import cma
import time

def fitness(x):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    return sum(x**2)

# serial evaluation of all solutions
def serial_evals(X):
    return [fitness(x) for x in X]

# parallel evaluation of all solutions
def _evaluate2(self, X):
    """redefine evaluate without the dependencies on neat-internal data structures
    """
    jobs = [self.pool.apply_async(self.eval_function, (x, )) for x in X]
    return [job.get(timeout=self.timeout) for job in jobs]
ParallelEvaluator.evaluate2 = _evaluate2
parallel_eval = ParallelEvaluator(12, fitness)

# time both
for eval_all in [serial_evals, parallel_eval.evaluate2]:
    es = cma.CMAEvolutionStrategy(10 * [1], 1, {'maxiter': 50})
    es.disp_annotation()
    while not es.stop():
        X = es.ask()
        es.tell(X, eval_all(X))
    es.disp()

In order to resume from previous offset, you have to use the same consumer group id when restarting a consumer, and commit offset regularly (client does it every 5s by default, but you should adapt it to your use case)

When you say you have a consumer for each partition, are you manually assigning the partition, or do you have three consumer in a same consumer groups which share the offset? In your case, you should use the same group id for each consumer, as you seem to want them to read all data from topics. Your call if you use subscribe (auto repartition) or assign (you decide which partition each consumer read).

If you assign the consumer to the partition, you should let the invalid offset for the assignment- this will use the stored offset for the consumer group id.

If the client crashed in the C code the signal would be SIGSEGV or SIGABRT, but SIGTERM is the default channel used by the `kill` utility, which could indicate some deliberate kill.
The Python client itself, nor the underlying librdkafka C libraries, does not send any signals.

Some random ideas:
 * Are you using any sort of soft watchdog in the Python process or supervisor that might send the SIGTERM signal?
 * Are you using any other library or module in the Python code that might do the same?
 * Check `dmesg` for hints, might be OOM (but I think that uses some other signal).

grid on
hold on
plot(cos(t), 'r')

%plot -s 200,150

b = 10*cos(t)+30; plot(b); grid on

a = [1,2,3]

b = a + 3;
```

In the MATLAB editor, this code could be evaluated all at once, by running the script. You can't do what you could in Jupyter, which is run each cell individually (say you only want to execute `b = 10*cos(t)+30; plot(b); grid on`)

I think it would be better if it would instead output:

```
%%
t = linspace(0,6*pi,100);
plot(sin(t))
grid on
hold on
plot(cos(t), 'r')

%% 
%plot -s 200,150

%%
b = 10*cos(t)+30; plot(b); grid on

%%
a = [1,2,3]

%%
b = a + 3;
```

This produces a neat way of then running the code in matlab, cell by cell in the same way as you can do in jupyter notebooks: place your cursor inside the cell and click _run section_ (or _Ctrl + Enter_). (Rather than running the whole script at once, you can run each cell individually).

Of course, it's possible to just manually add `%%` at the beginning of every cell in a matlab_kernel notebook (this is what I do at the moment) but if possible it would be great to have this automated.

I did, by the way, find one example of a somewhat annoying edge case:

```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from dateutil import tz

UTC = tz.tzutc()
NYC = tz.gettz('America/New_York')
hr = timedelta(hours=1)

before_dst = datetime(2015, 11, 1, 0, 30, tzinfo=NYC)

in_transition = before_dst + hr
print("Arithmetic as NYC: {}".format(in_transition.tzname()))           # EST
in_transition_via_utc = (before_dst.astimezone(UTC) + hr).astimezone(NYC)
print("Arithmetic as NYC: {}".format(in_transition.tzname()))           # EDT!
print("Arithmetic as UTC: {}".format(in_transition_via_utc.tzname()))   # EDT
```

This is a consequence of the fact that `before_dst` is specified directly with the fold-naive `NYC` tzinfo, then when `fromutc()` is first called on `NYC`, the object itself is modified. I did it this way to prevent proliferation of `tzinfo` instances, but I think it's better to just generate a shallow copy of `tzinfo` with the `_fold` status changed every time a `_fold` transition takes place.

You're right about the hold and cold analogy. Each point you add to a heatmap adds a certain heat. More points => more heat. The weight you give each point is the amount of heat. So even if points have a low weight, they still add heat. So if you use housing price as a weight, every point will add heat.

You're looking for something that averages the values of overlapping points. A heatmap doesn't make averages, but instead sums the values of overlapping points.

If you search for 'housing price heatmap' you will find most images are chloropleths:
https://www.google.com/search?q=housing+price+heatmap&tbm=isch

There is one example of a heatmap, but there they don't use the housing price as a weight, but the difference between asking price and amount paid. That leads to positive and negative numbers.
https://www.canadianbusiness.com/blogs-and-comment/check-out-this-heat-map-of-toronto-real-estate-prices/

So one solution is to create regions, calculate the average asking price per region and create a chloropleth.

Another is to transform your data into positive and negative weights. But keep in mind that more points => more heat.

The general problem here is really with UTA, the source of alignments used by hgvs.

The problem is that UTA assumes that transcripts are aligned exon-wise. When an exon spans an assembly gap or contains intervening sequence, the exon corresponds to two or more aligned regions. UTA doesn't accommodate that. hgvs attempts to recognize such cases and raise an exception, but it's possible we don't cover all of these cases.

Fixing the underlying issue is complex. See #385, biocommons/uta#198, and biocommons/uta#200.

It would be extremely helpful if you would add specific examples to this issue in a form roughly like this:

```
(venv) snafu$ hgvs-shell 
In [1]: %doctest_mode
>>> hgvs.__version__
'1.0.0.post4.dev1+h7c049c8b37e4.d20170414'
>>> str(hdp)
'UTA_postgresql <data_version:uta_20170117; schema_version:1.1; application_name=hgvs-shell; url=postgresql://anonymous:anonymous@uta.biocommons.org/uta/uta_20170117; sequences-from=seqfetcher>'
>>> v = hp.parse_hgvs_variant("NC_000001.10:g.16890441C>T")
>>> am37.g_to_c(v, "NM_017940.4")
...
HGVSInvalidIntervalError: start or end or both are beyond the bounds of transcript record
```

(`hgvs-shell` is installed with hgvs. It initializes a UTA dataprovider (hdp), Parser (hp), Validator (hv), and 37 and 38 AssemblyMappers (am37 and am38). This makes it easy for me to reproduce errors and incorporate them into our regression test.)

Someone else noted that you are opening the `tty` variant of the device. This may cause issues. pySerial is designed to work with raw, binary ports and some OS, e.g. from the BSD family, have two device entries for each serial port, one `tty` type for textual content and a `cua` or similarly named variant for binary communications. You need to use the later.

The problem seems to be this (at least for Windows): https://stackoverflow.com/a/18205006 . `cpu_info` internally creates a new process to encapsulate some code that is very likely to fail. A new process re-imports the original main file. Wrapping everything in if `__name__ == 'main'` solves the problem.

Regarding clients of libz3 and package managers. There are occasional asks for supporting package managers out of the box and I have to resist this as the setup complications, dependencies and moving parts would drag down main efforts. Having the build environment do end-to-end builds ensures that what comes out fits together. Sometimes Python and Java users have a stale libz3.dll somewhere and update their API bindings and they run into trouble. This generates GitHub issues, that call for attention.

There is another obstacle wrt OCaml from my point of view: it isn't well supported on Windows other than under Cygwin, which has its own barriers of entry for non-rudimentary uses. An Ubuntu shell is an option for me, but it is a separate setup cost whenever moving between machines (or reimaging them).

Hey @oruebel 

Sorry about the long delay in responding. I got busy with other things and am now getting back to this.

My use case is as follows: we are trying to stimulate a slice with light while its electrical activity is being recorded on a multi-electrode array. The electrode data (as well as associated digital and analog data) is recorded in a propriety file formats that we later convert to an HDF5 file.

During the experiment, we shine light of different patterns dynamically constructed, on the slice and want to observe the electrical response to these patterns. Consequently, continuously during the experiment, for every video frame shined on the slice we record a bunch of associated info to the hdf5 file. This is the streaming data I'm talking about.

* For example, for each video frame displayed, for every object on the screen, we save the intensity of this object at that frame.
* For each video frame we also output a n-bit digital signal on a device that is synced with the frame display and that n-bits of data is recorded by the electrophys recording system for post-hoc data alignment.
* There may be other data streams that we'd save to the file live, e.g. the start and stop time of a perfusion device.

So this data is continuously streamed. I'd like to be able to just keep streaming them to the pynwb hdf5 file as the data is created/acquired. Once done, I'd take our electrophys data and convert to pynwb from the propriety format and link to it from the streamed data file so all the data is in one place.

There's another aspect to this, I couldn't figure out how to save added data without saving the whole file again through calling e.g. io.write (but it's been a month since I tried last). E.g. imagine that I need to update some configuration data while the experiment is being prepared and want to autosave every couple of minutes. But this may be a separate issue.

Thanks for your patience!

:+1:  Likewise, if y values are listed explicitly, it would be nice if the `plot` method would plot only those y values and not other columns in the table.  Currently it is necessary to first subset the table, and then plot:

```py
tbl = Table([range(25), np.random.randn(25), np.random.randn(25), np.random.randn(25)], ["A", "B", "C", "D"])

# This plots A vs B, C, D, etc.., instead of A vs B.  That feels unexpected to me.  
tbl.plot("A", "B")

# meanwhile this feels redundant
tbl.select("A", "B").plot("A", "B")

```

As you probably know, `pandas` already has both the behavior David mentions and the behavior of plotting just the columns given in the `y` argument:

```py
## Plots just A vs B, 
tbl.to_df().plot("A", "B")

## Plots A vs B & C but not D
tbl.to_df().plot("A", ["B", "C"])

## Plots just B vs row number:
tbl.to_df().plot(y = "B")
```

Thanks to @faith0811 see https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/pull/766.

In short: pyzmq has a gc thread to handle the lifetime of zerocopy messages. This thread uses a zmq context. When you gevent monkey patch the threading API, the gc thread is not using the green wrapper of the zmq context (zmq.green.Context). The gc thead hangs.

The latest version of pyzmq does detect if the threading API was monkey patched, and uses zmq.green.Context in this case. This likely means you have to monkey patch the threading API early on, before pyzmq starts the gc thread.

No, Kinto doesn't have any concept of relationships or "foreign keys". In this, it is much like other document-store technologies like Redis, CouchDB or Mongo. The equivalent of a SQL join is to fetch related things on the client. You could do this in-memory, like you propose; this is easier if you've already "synced" the collections locally. If you're just using an HTTP-based client, you could also do it with multiple round-trips to the server.

I hope this answers your question; if it doesn't, feel free to reopen. This is a fine place to ask questions, but we also list several other places at http://kinto.readthedocs.io/en/stable/community.html :)

Sorry if I wasn't clear. For rollout and train steps, I guess this was more of an observation about the difference from the original DDPG paper. If you're taking N-steps, why not just use n-step backups (asynchronous updates?). It shouldn't really affect the results, but it's a difference from the original paper i think. (this is not the main concern)

The main concern with the DDPG evaluations is this. If people publish a paper using the evaluation as is, I'm not sure if it's proper. Taking rollouts which use a mixture of policies doesn't seem correct and could result in improper reporting. Let's look at an example.

Take 100 steps, train for 40 iterations.

**Case 1:**

Now, my policy knows very well how to handle this 100 steps. So we take these same 100 steps on the evaluation policy and move on. This is what is currently implemented (it seems).

**Case 2:**

*If*, however, we reset our evaluation environment and run 5 full rollouts, you may see the case that the policy forgets how to handle the previous 100 steps or doesn't yet know how to handle the rest of the policy so it'll fail.

In **Case 1** you've skewed your results in your favour by evaluating in sections instead of a single policy on a full rollout. You avoid the possible problem of catastrophic forgetting (if it is in fact there). By using a mixture of policies that were *just* trained on the sequence that you're about to evaluate on, you're not really evaluating the policy.

Does that make sense? I'm not sure if I'm right in my assumptions here, but you can see the difference if you look at the rllab implementation of DDPG.

                            info.parent_type,
                            info.field_asts[0].selection_set,

The way I would recommend is actually creating a `GraphQL` enum type, as is more powerful, semantic and takes the advantage of GraphQL enums that clients might find useful.

However, re-reading the issue, it seems is not actually the desired behavior.

You can achieve something similar by creating a new field type, and mapping from the protobuf to a `Int`.

```python
from functools import partial

class ProtoEnum(graphene.Field):
    def __init__(self, protobuf_enum, *args, **kwargs):
        self.protobuf_enum = protobuf_enum
        super(ProtoEnum, self).__init__(graphene.Int, *args, **kwargs)

    def resolver(self, parent_resolver, root, args, context, info):
        resolved_value = parent_resolver(root, args, context, info)
        # We check is a valid value in the protobuf enum
        # This might be not actually the way to check, but serves the concept!
        assert resolved_value in self.protobuf_enum
        return resolved_value

    def get_resolver(self, parent_resolver):
        return partial(self.resolver, parent_resolver)
```

Again, I think the cleaner and best way to achieve this would be using a `graphene.Enum` (as it maps to a GraphQL enum), and could be used not only in output fields, but also in input arguments.

It's not possible to scale the font arbitrarily to fill an area in ESC/POS.

As you've said, you can can switch between font A (12x24)  and B (9x17), and do any integer multiple of the size up to 8. You can end up with a lot of different column counts from this, but not 36. To get 36 columns, you would simply have to either adjust the print area, or prepare your output somewhere else (draw a PNG) and send it as an image.

You can adjust the print area by loading 58mm paper, enabling/disabling 42 column emulation mode to make the printer ignore part of the paper, or by directly adjusting the left margin and print width (I don't think these last two are not implemented in `python-escpos`, but pull requests would be welcome).

Luego de una breve investigación, pareciera ser que sí, es obligatorio codificar los espacios segun RFCs

## RFC 1738 (1994):
https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc1738.txt
>Unsafe:
  Characters can be unsafe for a number of reasons.  *The space
  character is unsafe because significant spaces may disappear and
  insignificant spaces may be introduced when URLs are transcribed or
  typeset or subjected to the treatment of word-processing programs.*
  The characters "<" and ">" are unsafe because they are used as the
  delimiters around URLs in free text; the quote mark (""") is used to
  delimit URLs in some systems.  The character "#" is unsafe and should
  always be encoded because it is used in World Wide Web and in other
  systems to delimit a URL from a fragment/anchor identifier that might
  follow it.  The character "%" is unsafe because it is used for
  encodings of other characters.  Other characters are unsafe because
  gateways and other transport agents are known to sometimes modify
  such characters. These characters are "{", "}", "|", "\", "^", "~",
  "[", "]", and "`".   All unsafe characters must always be encoded within a URL. For
  example, the character "#" must be encoded within URLs even in
  systems that do not normally deal with fragment or anchor
  identifiers, so that if the URL is copied into another system that
  does use them, it will not be necessary to change the URL encoding.

## RFC 3986 (2005)
Definitivamente los espacios en blanco no son parte de una URL válida. No figuran en la notación regular del Apéndice A, y se sugiere eliminarlos de todo input de usuarios:
http://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc3986.txt

>In practice, URIs are delimited in a variety of ways, but usually within double-quotes "http://example.com/", angle brackets <http://example.com/>, or just by using whitespace. These wrappers do not form part of the URI.
[...]
In some cases, extra whitespace (spaces, line-breaks, tabs, etc.) may have to be added to break a long URI across lines.  The whitespace should be ignored when the URI is extracted.
[...]
For robustness, software that accepts user-typed URI should attempt to recognize and strip both delimiters and embedded whitespace.

## Curso de acción sugerido
Según lo acordado con @beni por Slack, haremos que
* DataJson.validate_catalog siga marcando como inválidas las URLs con espacios, pero sugiera la corrección por %20.
* readers.read_ckan_catalog emita una advertencia cuando encuentre una URL con espacios en blanco, e incondicionalmente los reemplace por %20.

```
def ctc_loss(y_pred, y_true):
    with tf.name_scope("CTCLoss"):
        indices = tf.where(tf.not_equal(y_true, tf.constant(0, dtype=y_true.dtype)))
        values = tf.gather_nd(y_true, indices)
        shape = tf.shape(y_true, out_type=tf.int64)
        sparse_y_true = tf.SparseTensor(
            indices,
            values,
            shape
        )
        return tf.nn.ctc_loss(inputs=y_pred, labels=sparse_y_true, sequence_length=320)
```
