@jagerman At first glance, it does seem like the following should work:
```cmake
find_package(PythonInterp)
find_package(PythonLibs PYTHON_VERSION EXACT REQUIRED)
```

And I really really wish it did. It would have saved me so many headaches. The issue is that the two find modules work completely independently with only a common version number. But it's easy to install two Python distributions with the exact same version number (e.g. on Linux, you can have the system python and conda). In that case `FindPythonLibs` will fly off and and return the first library it finds matching the version number, but it's not going to care that it doesn't actually match the Python executable returned by `FindPythonInterp` (everything blows up at runtime because of the exe/lib mismatch). 

This is especially problematic on macOS because it's very common to install Python from homebrew instead of using the bundled system variant. CMake will happily find the homebrew exe because it's first in the path, but by default it will return the system lib, thus requiring manual intervention. It because even worse if you have 3 different distributions installed (system, homebrew, conda -- all needed for testing) in which case the default CMake scripts will really go nuts. This has not changed even with the newer CMake versions.

There is an additional layer to this on Windows where 32-bit and 64-bit variants of Python are still used. The default CMake scripts can again return a mismatched library because it only looks at the version -- `FindPythonInterp` and `FindPythonLibs` don't cooperate in any way. There is also the possibility of using the wrong compiler bitness (`FindPythonLibsNew` shows a nice error message in this case).

There are probably other issue here that I'm forgetting... or selectively erasing from memory because of how frustrating the whole thing was to deal with (for a cross-platform, Python-distribution agnostic build system).

`FindPythonLibsNew` resolves these problems beautifully -- asking the executable for the information is much better than going around it and searching various paths. The module is a bit outdated at this point (the Continuum Analytics copyright is from 2012) but the approach is still the correct one and it's much better to fix this one issue with it, rather than go back to the builtin CMake modules and dance around their many issues.

Sorry for the lengthy rant. Back to the issue at hand: the Python executable should have a good hint at where the library is. Perhaps `get_config_var('LIBPL')` or `get_config_var('LDLIBRARY')` might be more appropriate? These aren't very well documented unfortunately.

C++17 support can definitely wait for 2.2, but I think the current PR fixes an important issue that is likely going to come up again in 2.1's stable lifetime (given the improved Eigen support in 2.1).

Ok, I've figured this out.

Digging into the error a bit more, the server is rejecting the TLS Client Hello, because TLS v1.0 is unsupported on GKE 1.3.

How do we select the TLS version? In general this can be easily overridden in Requests (see [this guide](https://lukasa.co.uk/2013/01/Choosing_SSL_Version_In_Requests/). But Requests lets urllib3 choose the default TLS version, which is usually the best available; that in turn depends on the installed version of OpenSSL:

```
>>> import ssl
>>> ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2'
>>> ssl.OPENSSL_VERSION
'OpenSSL 0.9.8zh 14 Jan 2016'
```

Turns out on MacOS, the bundled OpenSSL version is antiquated. This also turns out to be much more of a pain to fix than expected; I'm using Python3.4, and Homebrew doesn't maintain pre-3.5 versions of Python3, so the following fix turned out to be the best for me: 

http://stackoverflow.com/a/37757206/37481

For posterity, reproduced here, assuming a fully-configured pyenv install:

```
brew update
brew install openssl
brew link --force openssl

CFLAGS="-I$(brew --prefix openssl)/include" \
LDFLAGS="-L$(brew --prefix openssl)/lib" \
pyenv install -v 3.4.4
```

Sadly it doesn't appear to be sufficient to install a new openssl; a new Python must be installed and linked against the new OpenSSL.

@sc-perth 
I saw the pull request #4195, and I thought I'd add a few comments/suggestions for the implementation (in part related to the suggestion made by @bmw )

**1.** As a user, I would easily understand that `--disable-log-rotation` disables log rotation, thus allowing it to grow infinitely (in the absence of other log rotators).
In contrast, the meaning of  `--max-log-count` set to 1 can be ambiguous. Besides what @bmw suggested, it can also mean (and that would be _my first thought_): rotate the log, but keep only one file. And then there is yet another ambiguity (related to "off-by-one): keep one old file or one file total?

Logical of at least some of the syslog rotators is that you configure the number of "_rotated_" (aka old) logs. E.g. FreeBSD newsyslog(8) uses an option `count`: "_count denotes how many rotated log files should be kept._ " See "Log Management and Rotation" with the example of newsyslog.conf(5) here:
https://www.freebsd.org/doc/handbook/configtuning-syslog.html

So, my suggestion would be:

- [ ]  keep `--disable-log-rotation` as the **only** way to disable rotation. 
- [ ] Rename  `--max-log-count` to e.g. `--max-old-log-count.` 
With that option set to 0 **and without** `--disable-log-rotation`, it would mean: rotate and discard all old logs (keep the latest one only).

I don't think this would require too many changes of the code, but this would make it much less ambiguous to the user (and hopefully even unambiguous).

**2.** While we are the topic of log rotation, let me share my initial experience about it. I was rather surprised when I saw multiple log files from one day, and some of them were very small. I ultimately understood that each file corresponded to each certbot command issued. E.g. "certbot certificates" generates a logfile that is only 995 bytes long. I understand that it is convenient from the point of view of debugging certbot. It is not very practical from the point of view of a production system.  So, my first inclination was to propose a `--max-log-size` option that would determine when the logs should be rotated.
I can see that the upcoming --disable-log-rotation in combination with the use of the system-based log rotators mitigates this situation. So, that seems to be alright.

**3** Wish-list: it would be nice to be able to configure the **level** of logging, especially when --disable-log-rotation is implemented. I thought it was possible, but I am not seeing it among the options (sorry, I didn't look into the source code): https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html . Am I missing it somewhere?

**4** Finally, regarding the question from @sc-perth about necessity of syslog alternative. I think it would be very useful and important for some very frequent use cases. Let me give just two reasons.
a) syslog can do more than just storing to a local file. It can do remote logging, it can throw messages to the screen of specific users or to  the console (does anybody have/use a console anymore? ;-)  ) ;
b) it allows separating messages with different levels (crit, error, info, debug, ...)
Many of those functions could be important for people who have multiple servers deployed and need to be alerted about, say, a problem with the cert renewal.
Those syslog functionalities exists on (all known to me flavors of) Unix, so that people do not invent new log analysis/management for each new application.
I am sure that you know all of this, but I thought I'd remind about it.

So, in conclusion, I think there are use cases that would benefit from certbot's own log files, and those that would benefit from the integration with syslog. So, it makes perfect sense to continue that integration. "May the Force be with you!" :-)

Glad you're interested in working on this! We're excited about getting mypy type annotations added to Certbot.

None of the core Certbot team has done much experimentation with mypy yet, however, dokazaki tried to work on this at another Python sprint and hit some issues. Basically, the problem is our continued support of Python 2.6. Certbot currently is supporting this old version of Python due to old Red Hat Enterprise Linux systems. Issue #2208 aims to remove our Python 2.6 support while still supporting old Red Hat systems, but there's some nontrivial engineering work to be done to make that happen.

mypy does not support Python 2.6. At the bare minimum, the `typing` module cannot be used. No one has done much additional probing to determine what other problems there are with using mypy on Python 2.6 (if there are any). If you'd still like to work on this knowing this hurdle, you have at least a few options:

1. Don't use `typing` at all. My understanding is this makes it next to impossible to provide meaningful type annotations.
2. When importing `typing`, put a `try` and `except` around each import.
3. Ignore problems with Python 2.6 and work on this in a separate branch. If you take this approach, tests on Travis will fail and we probably won't be able to merge the work into `master` for a few weeks, but it'll allow you to ignore the hurdles that should be going away soon.

Alternatively, maybe you have an even better idea!

Just to be clear, did you try this?

~~~
Salesforce().Account.upsert("myfield__c/http:%2F%2Fmysite.com%2Fid", {})
~~~

requests *should* leave the %2F's unchanged (this is a "reserved" character in RFC 2396).  Does it not?  If so, I can argue strongly that's a bug in requests.

Unfortunately, period is in the set of unreserved characters, so period and %2E are equivalent. RFC 2396 continues:

> Unreserved characters can be escaped without changing the semantics of the URI, but this **should not be done** unless the URI is being used in a context that does not allow the unescaped character to appear.

(my emphasis).  So if Salesforce really requires %2E, that's a Salesforce bug.

Basically I need a way to link task id, pbs job id or name, and sample id. If task fails, I need to know which sample fails, and sometimes which job fails. Currently `name` can be set to `ID` (if there is such info) to link sample id to job name, but there is no easy way to link task id to pbs job. So we can

1. Rename `name` so that task id is always the same as job name. (#842)
2. Add value of `name` to `tags` automatically to link sample ID to tasks.

I tend to remove `name` because
1. one less parameter, users do not need to worry about adding `ID` to `name` and/or `tags`.
2. task id is always the same as job name, which makes things a lot easier.

> when will we / will we not prompt for the name and description now?

In short:
 - If `Pulumi.yaml` has `name: ${PROJECT}` (like with all of our [templates](https://github.com/pulumi/templates)), we prompt.
 - If `Pulumi.yaml` has a project name already specified that isn't `"${PROJECT}"` (like with all of our [examples](https://github.com/pulumi/examples)), we don't prompt.

Of course it's a little more nuanced... We prompt when:
 - `--yes` has not been specified
 - `--name` has not been specified
 - `Pulumi.yaml` has `name: ${PROJECT}`

Similar story for `description` and `${DESCRIPTION}`.

The reason: we don't want to have to change all our examples to use `name: ${PROJECT}` and `description: ${DESCRIPTION}`. We want those to be git cloneable, buildable, and runnable on their own without using `pulumi new`. But we also want to be able to use (and encourage the use of) `pulumi new <url to example>` to make it easy to acquire an example and get it configured and deployed.

Does that help?

I can reproduce the error more simply without actors, e.g., just

```python
import ray
import gym
from ray.rllib.dqn import models
from ray.rllib.dqn import dqn

@ray.remote
def example_remote_function():
    env = gym.make("CartPole-v0")
    config = dqn.DEFAULT_CONFIG
    dqn_graph = models.DQNGraph(registry=None, env=env, config=config, logdir=None)

ray.init(num_cpus=1)

ray.get(example_remote_function.remote())

ray.get(example_remote_function.remote())  # The second one will fail.
```

Note that I'm using `num_cpus=1` so that the two remote function calls get executed by the same worker.

The key issue is that TensorFlow leaves some "state" on the worker process that then causes problem for the second function execution. This can be addressed in two ways.

1. Specify that the worker should exit after executing `example_remote_function` so that state doesn't leak between the tasks. This can be done by changing the remote decorator to `@ray.remote(max_calls=1)`.

2. You can also create the model inside of a new TensorFlow graph to avoid pollution of the TensorFlow global variables. E.g., change the remote function implementation to do something like

    ```python
    import tensorflow as tf
    with tf.Graph().as_default():
        dqn_graph = models.DQNGraph(registry=None, env=env, config=config, logdir=None)
    ```

I'm not sure if this kind of logic belongs into Streamlink. Streamlink's only job is to fetch stream data from streaming services and then do some stuff with that data.

I understand that the user activity status serves a purpose on the Twitch website, but it is totally unrelated to the functionality of Streamlink. IMO, this kind of stuff belongs into 3rd party applications. As a suggestion on my side, you could make a feature request on the Streamlink Twitch GUI, but I'm not sure if it's worth implementing it there, since the user activity is tied to the friends and chat system, which is not implemented there.

-----

Regarding a potential implementation:

The API endpoints which are used here are not part of the public Twitch API (v5), which is usually the case for stuff that's not considered stable yet or which changes a lot, since it's not meant for the public to be used. If you want to set a user's activity, you also need to be aware of the user's settings first, which includes two options for disabling updating the activity (user being invisible or prohibiting status updates).
`https://api.twitch.tv/kraken/users/:id/status`
`https://api.twitch.tv/kraken/users/:id/status/settings`

Watching multiple streams at once would also be a bit problematic if it was implemented into Streamlink directly. Each request requires a session ID, which is generated randomly and then SHA1'd and substr'd to a length of 16 chars. Since this session ID can not be shared between multiple streamlink processes, the user's activity would be overwritten by each Streamlink process with different data.

As for the analogy to GitHub, I see the following differences:
- GitHub launched when there was still no dominant resource for effective social coding, and has since established itself as the gold standard in code collaboration services.
- GitHub's continuing respect of, and contributions to, the OSS community has earned it a far greater level of trust from that community than many of its members would normally grant to a proprietary SAAS.
- The network effect is so highly relevant to this particular form of collaboration that, speaking practically, not using GitHub for an active OSS project is a disservice to that project's future potential.

None of these exceptions to the inherent tension between OSS and proprietary SAAS appears to hold true of manageacloud, however beneficial their product may actually be.

To my knowledge, this may be hard to do. Besides, if a user is simply installing from pypi without knowing which version of btcpy they're downloading and what its hash should be, they are still trusting that pypi hasn't been compromised and serving them a bad copy of btcpy.

One way to deal with this issue is to specify a list of btcpy versions and their corresponding hashes in a visible place independent from pypi.org (e.g. in the README file here). We should also make it known to users to only use the following installation idiom:

```
pip install chainside-btcpy==0.2 --require-hashes
```

Using this, pip will print out the remote hash, and the user will have an opportunity to verify that the remote hash matches the one in the README file, and will then have to specify it manually in order to proceed.

This is a trust issue that manifests quite often with pgp keys, where people choose to trust key directories without verifying key identities independently and through a secondary channel. Ultimately this type of security is hard to outsource and make "transparent" for end users, and eventually education is the only way to make sure that good security practices are enforced.

The ODC can just Index or it can Ingest (which is index+slice). The principle reason for ingestion is the data can be transformed from what is usually an archival data storage format into a computationally efficient form. With repeated use and tuned data storage forms the ODC can achieve higher overall performance compared with Index only.

How much of an improvement varies depending on the primary analytics use-cases, archive storage form and deployed environment. For example, the CSIRO deployment of the ODC on AWS ingests the archival form of the data (in most cases scenes or swaths in geotiff or netcdf) into AWS S3 object storage using the ODC S3 Array IO storage plugin. S3 is cheaper than file storage and with the S3AIO plugin scales better with increased computational nodes. The CSIRO deployment tends to treat the ingest form of that data as a caching mechanism where our most frequently used data is available. In future we would like to make the cache management automatic and on demand.

Alternately, the Digital Earth Australia deployment on the NCI High Performance Computing system has a shared archive of EO data. The data needs to be compatible with a range of non-ODC applications. The ODC uses this data in place as netcdf files via indexing only. It is efficient as the NCI HPC system has tuned these files to be performant in that environment whilst being compatible with a range of applications, of which ODC is one. There is no need to ingest in this deployment.

So sometimes, just indexing is easy, if you have a need for an archival form. The ODC will work with both index only and ingested forms. Which is more efficient depends on your environment and use-cases.

But, I've realized I was unclear about the big picture here – sorry about that! Let me try to explain the motivation for the `RunVar` thing.

In 0.3.0, we had `TaskLocal`, whose API was designed to be like `threading.local` but for tasks, and we had `RunLocal`, whose API was designed to be like `threading.local`, but for runs. This makes things easy, because once you know how to any of these you know how to use all of them. But now we're replacing `TaskLocal` with `ContextVar`, which ruins the symmetry. Solution: replace `RunLocal` with `RunVar` to keep things symmetric.

So getting back to the doc error: we should deprecate `RunLocal` and delete its docs entirely, which will certainly fix the error as a side-effect :-). Does that make sense?

To follow up re #88:

## Name
How about `name_func` for a name in the new implementation in #88 ? It's more concise, yet as explicit. 

## Implementation
### Function
It also should be defined as follows: `func(filename: str, ext: str) -> tuple`, where the returned value is a `tuple` of either `('unique_name.ext',)` or `('some', 'path', 'unique_name.ext')` that will be fed to `os.path.join` as: 
```python
os.path.join(MEDIA, *func_resp)
```
whose result must be a unique path. 

**Note**: *MEDIA* has to be enforced. It is a non-negotiable part because it enforces consistency and maintains security. Otherwise, inexperienced developers might feel inclined to save the file anywhere they feel like, which would be a recipe for disaster. 

### Fallback
Just in case, we can double check to ensure there is no existing file at the given path. If there is, we can **(a)** add some random number to the name, and  **(b)** raise a warning explaining that the path produced must be unique, but it is not.

You can install FMPy without an internet connection with PIP by downloading the [latest wheel](https://pypi.org/project/fmpy/#files). Copy it to the target machine and enter

```
pip install --no-deps FMPy-0.2.5-py2.py3-none-any.whl
```
or using Conda by downloading the respective [Conda package](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/fmpy/files)

```
conda install --no-deps fmpy-0.2.5-py36_0.tar.bz2
```

Depending on your use case you might need [additional packages](https://github.com/CATIA-Systems/FMPy/blob/master/docs/install.md#required-packages). The [Anaconda distribution](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) contains most of these packages out-of-the-box.

The low-level API (fmpy.fmi*) maps more or less directly to the FMI so it's very unlikely to change except maybe for bug-fixes. The conservative version numbering (0.x.x) was chosen to comply with [semantic versioning](https://semver.org/) and does not mean that we plan big changes to the API.

In the commits above I went ahead with dropping the default values for `initial_date` and `end_date` despite what was discussed earlier.
What convinced me otherwise was trying to do `api.query(aoi, ingestiondate='[... TO NOW]', ... other params ...)` instead of using the main date parameters and failing because of not paying attention to the default date values.
I'd argue that we should not add any parameters to the query by default. This is the most logical behavior in a sense. My main concern with removing the date parameter defaults was removing the effective query size limit from `query()`. I think this has been mitigated by the recent addition of a progressbar for long-running queries. As for the documentation value of the defaults, I think the docstring is pretty clear regarding the values that can be used there.
Do you agree?

I also added support for range values in `query()`, e.g. `query(cloudcoverpercentage=(0, 30))`, and extended the date validation and parsing to all known date types (such as `ingestiondate`) in addition to the main ones. Code reviews are welcome.

I updated the API to allow single-sided ranges to be used for non-date ranges as well, also allowing `None` to be used. In case both bounds are set to unlimited, the keyword will be removed from the query.

I also added a check for the presence of duplicate keywords in a query. This can happen due to capitalization (`beginposition` and `beginPosition`) or mixing function parameters and `**keywords` (`date` and `beginPosition`).

I've changed the name and merged the documentation of known errors into the master branch.

I want to note that current ONNX spec:
1) describes "math" part of computation that can be differently translated to the backend
2) points to axes numerically, not semantically. I.e. Concat op says 'axis=2' instead of 'axes=channels'. Semantical meaning of axes is only local to some ops like Conv.

This means that the global data layout format doesn't make sense in general - it doesn't apply to some ops and also modifies semantics of other ops in unexpected way.

There's also an aspiration of standartizing model representation. We either allow only one axis layout (in which case burden goes only to backends/frontends that support different ones) or we allow multiple axis layouts via flags/separate ops (in which case every backend would need to figure out how to handle all them). Thus I'm leaning to allow only one data layout in the serialized model.

In other words, serialized ONNX model should be represented in a single well-defined layout (which currently happens to be NCHW as more common one).

Given this, it seems that we need to have a set of transforms for changing the layout when importing/exporting to frameworks that have different one (like TF). What would it take to do it:

1) run a path through the graph and annotate edges which are coming from the input and thus have semantical axes assigned to them (i.e. figure out which tensors are 'images'). This can be done by having the information on inputs as you described

2) for tensor transformation ops like Concat or Flatten apply relevant transposition, e.g. replace axis=1 to axis=3

3) for ops like Conv replace them with some fake ConvNHWC that assumes different input layout and can be translated to the backend differently. One option is to add ConvNHWC as a legit op to ONNX but instead of requiring people to implement it directly, rely on the transformation pass to get rid of it for the backends that don't support different layout.

4) if there are some graph initializers that get transposed as the result we can do constant folding.

Those transformations need to happen when importing or exporting the model. On way is to put them in onnx-tf directly.

Another option is to put them in a shared place (onnx repo itself) and effectively have a prepackage tool that can take a model and change its data layout producing another model (with fake ops like ConvNHWC).

As you correctly noted, it's a non-trivial undertaking (maybe still simpler than LLVM :) ) but I feel that's the only valid way to address the issue systematically. Also, we started doing some scaffolding for graph rewrites in https://github.com/onnx/onnx-caffe2/tree/master/optimize-onnx with the goal to put it to the onnx repo eventually. We can consider putting this kind of rewriter on the same infra.

Hi iambibhas,

Thank you for showing your interest in this project! 
It is indeed like you said, the views are wrapped by a function in order to track the time it takes for the endpoint to execute. Inside this wrapper function, the gathered information is directly stored in the database by executing a query on that database.  Looking into making this persistence process a background job might indeed be a good suggestion for this project. 
As for the performance overhead of the Dashboard, we would like to refer to [this paper](https://github.com/flask-dashboard/ICSME-18-Paper/blob/master/paper.pdf), more specifically Section VIII. I hope this answers your questions. If there is anything else we could clarify or assist with, please do not hesitate to ask us.
